title: Route the requestQuality citation gate through the isotonic calibration model instead of raw cosine
problem: The good/weak/gap gate that drives citationPolicy never applies any calibration curve — it compares RAW normalized cosine against hard thresholds. `assessRequestQuality` reads `topScore = resolveCalibrationScore(results[0])` (confidence-scoring.ts:453), where `resolveCalibrationScore` (line 170-174) returns `resolveAbsoluteRelevance` = `Math.max(0, Math.min(1, similarity/100))` (pipeline/types.ts:90-97), a pure linear pass-through with no curve. The 'good' verdict fires at `topScore >= TOP_DOMINANT_THRESHOLD (0.8)` or `topScore >= HIGH_THRESHOLD (0.7)` with margin/ratio (confidence-scoring.ts:471-477), and `deriveCitationPolicy` maps `label === 'good'` -> `cite_results` (search-results.ts:368-370). Meanwhile a fitted isotonic model EXISTS and says the opposite: its highest bucket maps x=0.758 -> y=0.20 P(relevant) (confidence-calibration-model.json), but `assessRequestQuality` never calls `maybeCalibrate` (it is only called at confidence-scoring.ts:388 for the per-result `confidence.value`). So the off-corpus 'kubernetes' hit at cosine 0.78 (benchmark-results.md:71) clears 0.7 and cites a spurious 'Spec Memory Runtime Retention Cleanup' doc, even though the shipped curve already knew a 0.78 input is only ~20% likely to be relevant. The calibration that would have caught this is fit, committed, default-on, and simply not wired into the gate.
evidence: confidence-scoring.ts:453,467 (topScore from resolveCalibrationScore), :471-477 (good thresholds), :388 (maybeCalibrate only on per-result value), pipeline/types.ts:90-97 (raw cosine pass-through), confidence-calibration-model.json (x=0.758->y=0.2), search-results.ts:368-370 (good->cite), benchmark-results.md:71-72 (kubernetes 0.78 good vs authentication 0.50 weak)
proposal: Make `assessRequestQuality` compute its decision score by passing the absolute-cosine top score through `maybeCalibrate` (the same isotonic model used at line 388) before comparing to thresholds, OR add a calibrated-probability gate stage. Because the isotonic curve compresses everything into [0, ~0.2], the 0.8/0.7/0.4 cosine thresholds cannot be reused on the calibrated output — introduce P(relevant) thresholds (e.g. cite-iff calibrated P >= a tuned floor) and keep the raw-cosine band only as a secondary signal. Gate the new behavior behind a flag (mirroring SPECKIT_ABSOLUTE_RELEVANCE_CALIBRATION) so it can be A/B'd. Reuse the existing coupling-guard provenance check (confidence-scoring.ts:239-254) so an RRF-domain model is refused.
benefit: Closes the exact documented failure: a 0.78 off-corpus cosine would map to ~0.20 calibrated P(relevant) and fall below any reasonable cite floor, flipping kubernetes from 'good/cite' to 'weak/do-not-cite' while authentication (already weak at 0.50) is unaffected. The citation gate finally uses the calibration the system already paid to fit.
risk: The isotonic curve is fit on a label set with zero off-corpus negatives (see proposal 2), so its absolute P-values are themselves suspect — wiring it in without re-fitting could shift in-corpus 'good' verdicts and regress recall-driven citation. Must be flag-gated and validated against the held-out eval AND the new red-team set before default-on. Strictly sequence after proposal 2 or ship them together.

title: Re-fit the calibration on a label set that contains off-corpus hard negatives
problem: The committed isotonic model was fit from a labeled set with NO true negatives, so it has never seen the off-corpus spurious-match class it is being asked to suppress. `ground-truth.json` has 97 queries / 275 relevance rows, and every relevance grade is 1, 2, or 3 — there are zero grade-0 rows (verified by counting: grades {1:29, 2:119, 3:127}). The 6 queries tagged `category: 'hard_negative'` (ground-truth-data.ts:23 defines the type) are NOT off-corpus rejections — each one has a grade-3 (maximally relevant) positive doc, so they test hard RECALL, not rejection. The held-out eval binarizes with RELEVANCE_THRESHOLD default 1 (run-calibration-heldout-eval.mjs:34), so every labeled pair becomes relevant=1; the fit literally cannot learn 'high cosine but irrelevant' because that pattern is absent from the training data. The model's top bucket only reaches y=0.2 because the corpus-derived proxy is sparse at high cosine, not because it learned that high-cosine-off-corpus is bad. The module header itself flags this: 'a CORPUS-DERIVED PROXY ... NOT human-judged live memory_search traffic' (confidence-calibration.ts:8-14).
evidence: ground-truth.json grade histogram {1:29,2:119,3:127} (zero grade-0); 6 hard_negative queries each carry one grade-3 positive (queryIds 92-97); run-calibration-heldout-eval.mjs:34 (RELEVANCE_THRESHOLD default 1), :307-324 (ship-model fit on full set); confidence-calibration.ts:8-14 (corpus-derived-proxy disclaimer)
proposal: Extend the ground-truth set (or add a sibling negatives set) with off-corpus / cross-topic hard negatives: queries like 'kubernetes', 'authentication', and other terms whose nearest corpus doc is a spurious cosine match, each labeled relevance:0 against its top retrieved doc(s). Re-run `run-calibration-heldout-eval.mjs` to fit a new shippable model on the augmented set (it already supports SPECKIT_CALIBRATION_SHIP_MODEL output, lines 307-324) and stamp `fittedUnderAbsoluteRelevance: true`. The PAV isotonic fitter (confidence-calibration.ts:199-241) needs no change — only the data. Keep the existing grouped-by-query k-fold honesty gate (MIN_ECE_GAIN, allFoldsImproved).
benefit: The re-fit curve will bend DOWN in the high-cosine region for off-corpus inputs, so a 0.78 cosine on an unrelated doc maps to a low P(relevant) for real. This is the data fix that makes proposal 1's wiring trustworthy and is the prerequisite for any per-class or hard-negative-aware variant.
risk: Hand-labeling off-corpus negatives is judgment-laden and small-N; a handful of negatives can over-bend the high-cosine tail and start rejecting legitimate strong in-corpus hits, regressing recall. Mitigate by validating in-corpus recall@k on the existing ground-truth queries before/after the re-fit (capture baseline first per the regression-baseline rule) and by keeping negatives proportionate to positives.

title: Add a standing off-corpus red-team eval so calibration drift is caught automatically
problem: The kubernetes false-relevance was found by a one-off 144-cell model benchmark, not by any repeatable gate, and the benchmark itself flags this as a measurement gap: 'a single term does not measure the rate. A standing red-team query set would' (029 implementation-summary.md:109) and 'Whether the off-corpus gap-detection result warrants a standing red-team query in the eval harness' is left open (029 spec.md:192). There is no automated test that asserts off-corpus terms earn weak/gap + do_not_cite. The existing `absolute-relevance-calibration.vitest.ts` only tests that STRONG cosine reads as good — it has no negative-direction case asserting a spurious high cosine is rejected. So any future re-tune of the 0.8/0.7/0.4 thresholds or the calibration curve could silently reintroduce the over-citation with nothing red.
evidence: 029 implementation-summary.md:109 ('a single term does not measure the rate. A standing red-team query set would'), 029 spec.md:192 (open question on standing red-team), absolute-relevance-calibration.vitest.ts:83-97 (only strong-positive cases, no off-corpus negative)
proposal: Add a red-team eval/test set of off-corpus and adversarial-vague queries with expected requestQuality in {weak, gap} and citationPolicy = do_not_cite_results, asserted against live search (or a fixed fixture of the retrieved rows). Wire it as a vitest case alongside absolute-relevance-calibration.vitest.ts and/or as a driver next to the held-out eval, with a pass gate (e.g. >= N% of off-corpus terms must NOT cite). Seed it from the 029 benchmark's off-corpus rows (kubernetes, authentication) plus new terms.
benefit: Turns a one-shot finding into a regression guard: the kubernetes class becomes a permanent assertion, so re-fits (proposals 1-2) are validated to actually fix it and future threshold changes that reopen the hole fail CI instead of shipping.
risk: Asserting against LIVE search makes the test corpus-dependent and flaky as the DB changes; prefer a frozen fixture of retrieved rows + cosine for the assertion, refreshed deliberately. A live variant should be a separate, non-blocking smoke driver.

title: Document that 'absolute-relevance calibration' is an axis swap, not a calibration curve, and fix the misleading naming
problem: The codebase uses 'calibration' for two unrelated things, and the conflation is what hides the bug. `resolveAbsoluteRelevance` and the SPECKIT_ABSOLUTE_RELEVANCE_CALIBRATION flag perform NO calibration in the statistical sense — they only swap the input axis from RRF-fusion magnitude to raw cosine (pipeline/types.ts:90-97; the test header at absolute-relevance-calibration.vitest.ts:10-14 confirms the whole point is 'reads cosine instead' of RRF). The ENV_REFERENCE entry reinforces this: SPECKIT_ABSOLUTE_RELEVANCE_CALIBRATION is described as 'Calibrate ... on an absolute cosine relevance signal' (ENV_REFERENCE.md:240) — but it is just `similarity/100` with no curve. The actual fitted curve lives under the separately-named SPECKIT_CONFIDENCE_CALIBRATION (ENV_REFERENCE.md:238). A reader sees 'ABSOLUTE_RELEVANCE_CALIBRATION = true' and reasonably assumes the cosine-to-relevance mapping is calibrated; it is not, which is precisely why a 0.78 cosine is trusted at face value in the gate. This is partly a doc/naming issue — lower priority than the code fixes — but it is load-bearing for anyone reasoning about the gate.
evidence: pipeline/types.ts:90-97 (raw similarity/100, no curve), absolute-relevance-calibration.vitest.ts:10-14 (subject is cosine-vs-RRF axis), ENV_REFERENCE.md:240 (calls the axis-swap 'Calibrate') vs :238 (the real fitted isotonic model under a different flag)
proposal: Clarify in ENV_REFERENCE.md (line 240) and in the pipeline/types.ts:80-89 doc-comment that SPECKIT_ABSOLUTE_RELEVANCE_CALIBRATION is an axis/scale selection (cosine vs RRF), NOT a fitted calibration, and cross-reference SPECKIT_CONFIDENCE_CALIBRATION as the only fitted curve. If proposals 1-2 land, update the wording to state which path is now calibrated. Optionally introduce a clearer alias for the flag while keeping the old name as a deprecated synonym.
benefit: Removes the false mental model that the cosine gate is already calibrated, making the proposal-1 gap discoverable and preventing a future maintainer from re-deciding 'calibration is on, the gate is fine' the way this bug slipped through.
risk: Pure doc/comment change — negligible risk. Only caveat: if the flag is renamed rather than just documented, ensure the old env var keeps working to avoid breaking deployed configs that set it.
